cross_connections.display_label: Emma Hart Willard invited her to Troy Seminary and she stayed on to teach music and drawing.

narrative_blocks.header: She taught music and drawing at Troy Seminary
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Porter Hart was one of the Berlin girls invited to Troy Seminary by Emma Hart Willard, her relative, at Willard's own expense. She stayed on to teach music and drawing at the seminary. She later married and became Mrs. William Dodd of Cincinnati.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Troy Female Seminary on Emma Willard's invitation; stayed on to teach music and drawing.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The brick house he built c.1800 at 203 Hudson Street, Berlin CT — four corner chimneys, still standing.

narrative_blocks.header: Cabinet-maker who built his own brick house
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Hart was a cabinet-maker in Berlin, Connecticut. Around 1800 he built a brick house at 203 Hudson Street, notable for chimneys at each of its four corners — each with a corresponding fireplace inside. In 1813 he purchased the tavern at Boston Corners and became its landlord.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter Emma Hart married her son Howard Hart White

sources.short: Hart Article (Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, NYGB Record)
sources.notes: Written by H00598 Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, age ~91, published in New York Genealogical and Biographical Record. Full text preserved in EH entry notes.

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)
Revolutionary Soldiers of the Town of Berlin (Berlin-Peck Memorial Library)

narrative_blocks.body: Serving as a lieutenant in General Wolcott's detachment, Samuel Hart was wounded at Saratoga on September 19, 1777, and two years later led the Berlin company that turned out at the New Haven alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: A thinker who could not speak unwritten
narrative_blocks.body: A great reader, he could not address an audience without first writing out his ideas; in religion he turned Universalist after a church dispute.

notable.primary_url_label: Berlin in the French and Indian War (p. 13)
notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary captain, father of Emma Hart Willard

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.

documents.document_blurb: Emma Willard's 1855 poem names both parents — the most direct portrait of Samuel Hart's character.
Almira Hart Phelps article on the Hart family, NYGB Record.

military_service.unit: Col. Cook's Regiment; Berlin militia company
military_service.notes: Lieutenant under Gen. Wolcott's detachment at Saratoga, wounded 19 Sep 1777; captain of the Berlin company at the New Haven alarm, 1779, repelling Tryon's invasion.
Fifteen days, 8-22 Aug 1757, in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry.

career.role: First Clerk and Treasurer, Berlin Congregational Church
career.notes: Withdrew from the Second Congregational Church in 1807 after a religious dispute.

cross_connections.action: Research Norton-Barlow family connection in Guilford records

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of poet-geologist James Gates Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart Percival's son James Gates Percival (born September 15, 1795) became a major American poet and the official state geologist of both Connecticut and Wisconsin after his Yale 1815 graduation. Elizabeth herself was described as 'by nature of a susceptible and delicate organization' who 'seems to have imparted to her son these qualities, with a tendency to excessive mental development.' She was understood to have transmitted depression and extreme sensitivity to her sons — James also suffered from depression throughout his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost husband and daughter within five weeks, 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth's first husband Dr. James Percival Jr. died January 22, 1807 in Kensington Connecticut at age forty, and five weeks later her daughter Harriet died February 16, 1807 at age seventeen. The compressed loss of husband and eldest child within five weeks marked the family's catastrophic early-1807. Elizabeth waited five years before remarrying — to Samuel Porter on April 6, 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James worked Porter farm before re-entering Yale
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1812 second marriage to Samuel Porter, Elizabeth's son James Gates Percival worked on Porter's farm before re-entering Yale to complete his 1815 degree. The interim farm labor between the family rebuild and resumption of Yale studies was the kind of practical stop-gap that interrupted many early-Republic professional-track educations after parental deaths reset family finances.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Kensington 1769, four children with Dr. Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart was born in 1769 in Kensington Connecticut (baptized December 24, 1769) and married Dr. James Percival Jr. there in January 1790 at age twenty. Four children survived to be documented: Harriet (died 1807 age 17), Edwin (died Troy NY), James Gates Percival (Yale 1815 poet-geologist), and Oswin Hart Percival (baptized 1809, died never married at a New Haven hospital). Elizabeth herself died around 1846.

INST066.name: Troy Female Seminary (Emma Willard School)
INST066.notes:
Founded 1814 by Emma Hart Willard (H00597) in Middlebury, VT as the Middlebury Female Seminary — the first women's higher education institution in the United States. Moved to Troy, NY in 1821 and renamed Troy Female Seminary. Renamed Emma Willard School in 1895. Willard resigned leadership in 1838 to her son John Willard (H01550). Almira Hart Phelps (H00598) was vice-principal; wrote Lectures on Botany (1829) during this tenure — 10,000 copies sold in three years. Twelve or more Berlin CT girls attended on Willard's invitation and expense, including Jane Porter Hart (HD2963) and Harriet Hart (HD2964). As of 2026: endowment $119M; grades 9-12 plus postgraduate; #1 private school Upstate NY 2018.
[former/alt detail] Formerly: Middlebury Female Seminary (1814); Troy Female Seminary (1821). Now: Emma Willard School.
